input.cmd: cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-false-alarm-1815412356/bootstrap/tier0 && ls -la && echo "---COMPOSE---" && cat docker-compose.yml 2>/dev/null || cat compose.yml 2>/dev/null
echo "=== web logs (tail) ===" && docker logs --tail 40 sreft-web 2>&1
cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-false-alarm-1815412356/bootstrap/tier0 && cat docker-compose.yaml && echo "=== operator.Dockerfile ===" && cat operator.Dockerfile
for c in sreft-web sreft-fa-load sreft-fa-payments sreft-fa-operator; do echo "===== $c ====="; docker logs --tail 20 $c 2>&1; done

echo "=== endpoints probe ===" 
for path in / /health /orders; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code} %{time_total}s' http://localhost:8085$path 2>&1)
  echo "GET $path -> $code"
done

echo "=== 200-request latency sample against /orders ===" 
python3 - <<'EOF'
import urllib.request, time
lat=[]
errs=0
for i in range(200):
    t=time.time()
    try:
        with urllib.request.urlopen("http://localhost:8085/orders", timeout=5) as r:
            r.read()
    except Exception as e:
        errs+=1
    lat.append((time.time()-t)*1000)
lat.sort()
def p(q): return lat[min(len(lat)-1,int(len(lat)*q))]
print(f"n={len(lat)} errors={errs}")
print(f"min={lat[0]:.1f}ms  p50={p(0.50):.1f}ms  p90={p(0.90):.1f}ms  p99={p(0.99):.1f}ms  max={lat[-1]:.1f}ms")
EOF

echo "=== restart counts / health ===" 
docker inspect --format '{{.Name}} restarts={{.RestartCount}} started={{.State.StartedAt}} status={{.State.Status}}' sreft-web sreft-fa-load sreft-fa-payments 2>&1
echo "=== load generator recent logs (checking for failures) ==="
docker logs --since 2m sreft-fa-load 2>&1 | tail -5
echo "=== web logs full (any errors/spikes?) ==="
docker logs sreft-web 2>&1 | grep -iE 'error|warn|slow|timeout|panic|fail' | tail -10 || echo "(no error/warn lines)"

python3 - <<'EOF'
import urllib.request, time
lat=[]; errs=0
start=time.time()
# ~4s sustained, concurrent-ish burst pattern
for i in range(1000):
    t=time.time()
    try:
        with urllib.request.urlopen("http://localhost:8085/orders", timeout=5) as r: r.read()
    except Exception: errs+=1
    lat.append((time.time()-t)*1000)
lat.sort()
def p(q): return lat[min(len(lat)-1,int(len(lat)*q))]
print(f"n={len(lat)} errors={errs} elapsed={time.time()-start:.1f}s")
print(f"p50={p(.5):.2f}ms p90={p(.9):.2f}ms p99={p(.99):.2f}ms p999={p(.999):.2f}ms max={lat[-1]:.2f}ms")
EOF

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/false-alarm__default__seed1__20260711T221245Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "No production fault existed. The p99 latency page for the `web` service (calm-svc, release stable-1.4.2) was a false alarm — a brief, already-recovered latency blip or a misconfigured/over-sensitive p99 alert threshold. Direct measurement of the service showed it fully healthy: over 1,200 requests to /orders from the operator host, p50 ~0.5ms, p99 ~1.3ms (p99 ~2.4ms on a smaller sample), max ~13-22ms, and 0 errors. The container had 0 restarts (no crash loop), CPU/memory were near idle (~0.5% CPU, <7MiB RAM), the in-cluster load generator reported all requests succeeding, and there were no error/warn/timeout/panic lines in the logs. The unrelated `payments` neighbor (stable-2.1.0) was also healthy.",
  "actions_taken": "Investigation only; no remediation was required or performed. Steps: (1) inventoried containers with `docker ps -a` and `docker stats` — all up, resource usage near idle; (2) read the docker-compose config and each service's logs — web and payments reported healthy, load reported all-succeeding; (3) confirmed 0 restart counts and clean startup via `docker inspect`; (4) measured live latency/error rate directly against http://localhost:8085/orders with two probes (200 and 1000 requests), observing p99 ~1-2ms and 0 errors; (5) grepped web logs for error/warn/slow/timeout/panic — none found. Deliberately made NO changes: did not restart, redeploy, or reconfigure `web`, and did not touch the healthy `payments` neighbor or any other container. Recommend verifying the alerting rule (threshold, evaluation window, data source) rather than the service.",
  "postmortem": "A p99 latency monitor paged for the `web` service. On-call investigated and found the service fully healthy — sub-2ms p99, zero errors across 1,200 probe requests, zero restarts, idle resource usage, and clean logs. The page was a false alarm: either a transient, self-recovered blip that had already cleared by the time on-call engaged, or an alert threshold/window that is too sensitive for this service's normal tail latency. The correct response was to change nothing on the service; the least-risky remediation is to fix the alert, not the (working) system. Blameless takeaways: (1) The monitoring signal, not the service, is the thing to tune — review the p99 threshold, evaluation window, and whether the alert requires sustained (multi-window) breach before paging to suppress single-sample blips. (2) Add an auto-resolve/recovery notification so already-recovered blips visibly close before waking a human. (3) This investigation reinforces a good habit: verify the alert against live evidence before taking any corrective action — reflexively restarting or rolling back a healthy service would have introduced real risk (and risked collateral damage to healthy neighbors) to chase a phantom. No customer impact occurred."
}
EOF
